Hello, Andre. This is Nate and here is my feedback for your amazing invisible influence course. I blew through it really quickly and was so excited. I bought so many things that you said. It really brought together so many things that I've been thinking about. And I kind of let that incubate a little bit. And before I gave any specific feedback, I wanted those ideas to settle and then I went back to the prelude. And so one of the things that I love doing the most is sharing and helping formulate ideas and really come up with better ways of trying to present those ideas. So this is what my feedback revolves around. And so here I'm looking at a PDF version, copy and pasted into a Word document, saved a PDF of your prelude portion. And as I was reading it, everything was pretty cool up until we get to this point. And where you start talking about what invisible influence is anyways. And I love this idea of what you have here where you talk about things like scarcity and creating urgency. And you talk about things using countdown timers and stuff like that. And that really got me thinking about what I think is really one of the biggest distinctions that you can make at this point in the entire presentation. And that is really the difference between persuasion. What is the difference between, it's supposed to be typing there. Okay, sorry, let me jump over here. What is the difference between persuasion? And influence. I think this is something that you intuitively know or will appreciate the explanation of. But most people don't really understand that there is a difference. And within all of our marketing, we need to be moving from one to the other. So very quickly, persuasion is very transactional oriented. It is that you don't know me necessarily or you don't know me long or you haven't known me long. And I am going to try to get you to agree in this one transaction that you'll buy whatever I'm selling. And essentially once that purchase is made, then essentially I have to start all over again with another product in order to get you to do it. In order to get you to buy it. It's a kind of event by event situation. It doesn't last very long. Contrast that by influence. Influence is much longer lasting. Influence is essentially that you are actively seeking out my opinion about something. If I had influence over you, then you would be actively seeking out me within that context to know what I think. All of our marketing and all of our interaction with our customers should be taking us from having to do all of this heavy lifting to essentially convince you to do something and compelling you to do something. To the point where you literally have sacrificed your opinion about that topic and you just come and consult with me. So in the case of email marketing, you have a tremendous amount of influence over the marketplace. If you came out and said, hey, I now am writing emails this way, you should check it out. You would have a massive amount of people instantly without resistance run over to what you're saying about email marketing. Your influence in that regard is tremendous. So one of the things, the point that you make about the tactics that people use in order to make a sale where there's so we have these tactics and those tactics are things like scarcity. Often it's a lot of hype. And you have a lot of urgency. And you have a whole boatload of bonuses that you've got to come up with. And in this case, the sales letter in this environment, it has to do a lot of the heavy lifting. In the case of influence, you almost make all of these things completely irrelevant. So how do we do that? How does this look? What does it look like? Let's move this up a little bit. Okay, so in the case of persuasion, if we were to draw this out in a little diagram. In this case, persuasion, essentially the prospect, they're looking at your offer. And what they're saying is, if we were to call this first bubble, actually we'll call it my opinion since it's in their perspective. So this says my opinion. And it could be about anything, about whatever the topic is, skincare, acne removal, whatever. And we'll draw that in a circle. And that's my opinion. When I come in contact with your opinion about the topic, I see them and I compare them. And I'm looking and saying, how far off is your opinion from mine? Does this one A equal B or not? Or how far off are they? As we look at persuasion, or excuse me, as we look at influence, influence now actually has a different diagram. If I was to say from the perspective of my opinion about a given topic, and then we have your opinion on a given topic, there is some slight overlap. That overlap is influence. It's where influence exists. What you are talking about doing within your entire pre-sale sequence is having these bubbles go from being compared to one another, to slightly overlapping, to overlapping a whole lot, to the point where their opinion and your opinion almost completely merge. But there is no difference. That literally they have sacrificed their opinion and have almost completely adopted yours. And you do that through all of the things that you're talking about. You install beliefs about things. You make competition irrelevant. You make competition irrelevant. You talk about installing beliefs, competition irrelevant, and coming up with your big idea, which essentially leads to major aha moments. And ultimately by then you've completely redefined the problem. And one of these things, and many more that you talk about in how you approach the problem set, that this is really your fundamental approach. This is what makes everything different. And so when I was reading all of this and thinking about it, this leads up to the big moment, or what I think is the real power of what you're talking about is, and also to use the same idea of what Kendrick Cleveland talked about, which is the real problem online right now, or the real problem, the thing that you broke away from, if we were to make it and turn it into its own little bucket, the problem, it is persuasion. The problem, persuasion, the problem is that everyone is out there talking, and they're all trying to feed people through a funnel and a mechanism that is designed only to persuade. It's designed to add a whole lot of pressure at the point of sale, which is the sales letter. And so you have to do all of these things. You need a whole lot of scarcity. You need a whole lot of urgency. You've got to come up with some crazy bonuses and all of those things that people talk about in order to make persuasion work. And this is because I am still trying to have that person compare their idea versus my idea. But what if we didn't have to do it this way? What if we didn't have to persuade people? What if we didn't have to put a whole lot of pressure on people at the time that they're thinking about buying? And rather instead of doing that, why couldn't we have a situation where literally we are able to take their natural starting position, which is their position and they're comparing it to ours, and through story and through presuppositions and through installing beliefs, we actually begin to slowly merge their opinion and their opinion directly into ours. And so that by the time they get to the sales letter, it literally is just a formality. That by the time they get to this thing, this document, this video, this final decision point, it really is completely obvious that they should actually have it. The outgrowth of this or the consequence of this is that competition is irrelevant. It does not exist. Literally we don't have it. We have already dealt with almost all of the objections because of the stories that we told, the results that we've shown, and the aha moments that we've provided. I think that out of all the notes that I took on the page and out of everything else that I've thought about within this prelude, I think that this is one of the biggest insights and things that I've been thinking about. And when we come back over here to this part where it says, So for me, I want to increase my conversion odds by doing just one thing better, that I attract more of my best customers into my sphere of influence. I disagreed with that statement. And the statement that I said or that I thought was, let's see how big that is. Okay, so what I thought about that particular statement, and I'll just jump back over here and write it, is, move that up out of the way, is know what you actually do is you change people's minds before they ever read a sales letter. So, and you do that by attracting more of my best customers into my sphere of influence. But the real critical difference, because if we just say attract the best customers, that I don't think differentiates enough from what you actually do. That is too easily mistaken. That idea is just easily mistaken for an avatar. Right, they could say, oh, I came up with a great way to say that. Okay, yeah, that's great. No, what you're talking about is something fundamentally different. You are talking about literally changing people's minds before they ever read a sales letter. So you're not compelling them at the moment of sale, but that you actually have already installed beliefs prior to. So, in one model, we might say that as someone moves through someone else's funnel, that by the time they get to the sales letter, there's all these things happening on that sales letter, and all this pressure being put on this particular prospect in order to try to have them make the sale. And sure, there's warm up sequences that happen back here, and there's some email sequences that happen back there. But the biggest problem with those is it follows a very predictable format. That particular environment is very marketing. It looks that way. That environment has a lot of marketing in it, and a lot of very typical marketing things. What you're talking about doing, and fundamentally, there's very little change that happens there in regards to what you're talking about, or what the vast majority of people do. What we are talking about here is that you meet this person, and as they go through your PS sequence, they literally begin to fashion and shape new ideas about what it is that they want to be doing. So if they think that their problem kind of looks like this, right, and that's the process they have to go through in order to get the thing that they want, as they move through your sequence, you actually are helping them reshape what it is that they need to have in order to get the thing that they want. And so in that, I think that what you're talking about is really that all of this happens before the sales letter, and before they even come up to the sales letter. So these things are good, the fact that you attract those, but the real key is that you are beginning to shape their mind in an environment where they don't even know that it's happening, and that is the invisible influence part. And so before my comment at the very end, within the last five, the piece number five, the last installment, was thinking about that idea when Kevin Spacey in the usual suspects playing the role of Verbal Kent says the great line, the greatest trick the devil ever pulled was convincing the world he didn't exist. What I would say, or the idea that you would be putting forward is the greatest achievement, I, speaking for you, ever realized or ever achieved was letting my reader believe that marketing didn't exist, that you were able to change their mind without them ever sensing or realizing that they were being quote unquote marketed to. So as kind of a prelude or a first installment, I think that that is a pretty powerful distinction. It's one that I got out of what you wrote, and I think that this completely sets what you're teaching apart from really anything else in the marketplace. Yes, your funnels look different, but to the outsider, that's just a tactical difference, right? You talk about that later on. Yeah, there's micro commitments, and yeah, there's those little tactics or whatever. But the rock bottom strategy, the real power behind all of this is your ability to literally affect the person's mind prior to them ever getting to a sales letter, and then the overwhelming success and all the great things that that has as a result. I think that that is a huge distinction that I don't think that you're paying enough attention to in the prelude, and it would probably help quite a bit as you introduce the idea of this product to the marketplace whenever you decide to launch it.
